reviews.review_text:
Plays like Tekken. I like the roster. Mechanics feel fine. 

Game is AGGRESIVELY monetized. DLC prices are ridiculous, even on sale, can't even test locked characters in practice. Full priced game trying to shill battle passes.

This much greed gets the downthumb.
If you like gambling, this game is it. Also, why are the DLCs almost the cost of the entire base game

Game itself is fine, not perfect, but people grossly overexaggerate its problems nowadays. If you don't take it too seriously, you'll have a fun time learning and fighting others. Just don't interact with the community. It's the most toxic corner of the FGC I've ever seen (and I used to play Dead By Daylight).
Going back to 7. The monetization and post launch content and balancing in this game is dog water.
Great graphics and a good online,🏷️but it has too many strong characters. You should nerf the strong characters to balance the online gameplay.

Well... It's been a while I've been entering the world of Tekken. The last time I play this series was on Tekken 5 and 6 back in 2010's and had a few experience in TAG Tournament and Tekken 7. Couple of weeks ago this game went on sale and I've manage to buy it and some say that this game had a brutal review bombing at the time I purchased it. So I let this game simmer for a while until it was optimized to the point it become playable once more...

Now here's a problem with the recent Tekken game that I personally found: It has a lot of "nerfing". Unlike on how Tekken 5 and 6 remain consistent, Tekken 8 needs a patch of update to make the game appeals to the player's input, which I found it annoying when you just want to improve the way you play the game at it's core without having yourself constantly felt FOMO for no good reason before couple of days later you rant and complains a lot about how bad the recent update is. This is why a consistency of a game in a old franchise like Tekken matters, because it has a fans from a various generations of both old and new worldwide.

As how this game improves, it is actually something new but refreshing to me. I'm an Asuka Kazama's main back since Tekken 5 and there's still a mechanic that I remembers a lot that I can use again, which is a good news to me. The thing that seems not familiar with is the new "burst" mechanic and the new way to juggle your enemy with a longer and damaging combos, which I need to learn more just to keep my muscle memories going as I play the game (I'm a bit of a slow-learner btw).

But y'know, there is no specific point on how you play Tekken. And since this game always connected with E-sport like Street Fighter 6 and Dead or Alive 6, competitiveness is the one thing matters now, leaving us a question either you're "very good" on playing Tekken or "very bad". Now, I'm an casual gamer and I found that making the game far too competitive in any shape and form was just not for me, which erased the term "fun" in the game in general and look more like an unstoppable addiction on getting better, faster and powerful on playing a game.

Whoever find this review who is not very good in playing this game, a casual gamer, plot lover or just plainly having fun, make sure you like the game and enjoy it. I'm still recommend this game as a fighting game to play.

Verdict: 8.5/10

Tekken 8 is a fantastic fighting game and, in my opinion, a clear improvement over Tekken 7 in many ways. The gameplay feels smoother, the Heat system adds a new layer of aggression without completely losing the depth the series is known for, and the visuals are some of the best I've seen in the genre. The roster is strong, matches are exciting, and learning new characters feels more rewarding than ever.

The game also does a great job of making Tekken more accessible to newcomers while still giving veterans plenty of room to master advanced mechanics. Whether you're playing casually or grinding ranked, the core gameplay is incredibly fun.

Unfortunately, I can't fully recommend it because of the DLC pricing and overall monetization. A lot of content is locked behind expensive DLC, and the way additional characters and other content are sold leaves a sour taste. It's disappointing because the game itself is genuinely excellent, but the cost of keeping up with everything feels unreasonable. This has been a common point of criticism among players despite the strong gameplay.

If you're only interested in the base game, you'll probably have a great time. But if you want the complete experience, be prepared to spend far more than the initial purchase price.

Hey, returning Tekken player here. Use to play Tekken 3 and 5 in arcade. Love this game but only problem is that I can't practice against DLC characters. At least you can make DLC characters open for "practice mode" so that we can learn to defend against them.

As someone who has spent a lot of time in fighting games, I can confidently say that Tekken offers one of the deepest and most rewarding combat systems out there. Every character feels unique, movement and spacing matter, and improving your skills is genuinely satisfying. The game is easy to enjoy casually, but mastering it takes dedication and knowledge. Great visuals, excellent character design, and endless replay value. Whether you're a beginner or a competitive player, Tekken is a game you can keep coming back to for years. Highly recommended. 🔥👊
Fun to play but people who use special style are satanic and fans of the minion movies. Also applies to Law players

This game will not boot up for the life of me no matter how hard i try so i will just let beautiful tall women at my local beat the ♥♥♥♥ out of me while i never practice because they deserve to beat a chump like me to feel good
I enjoy playing and learning the games mechanics, and the competition it breeds. I stepped away from it to dela with college only to find out they improved defense and tech to my liking.

Tekken 8 has taken a turn for the worst encouraging braindead aggression and encouraging skilless defense/comeback mechanics like rage arts and safe high power crushes. I miss the good days of tekken from tekken 5 to tekken 7. Unfortunately, the game keeps getting dumbed down with each iteration. I feel like Tekken is starting to lose its skill expression. Been playing for years and reached God of Destruction with Steve.

played it for a little when it came out and then picked it back up when they added Armor king to the game 
Been playing on and off since then and it seems like the game gets worse everytime i come back 
dont know if id recommend it even if it was on sale

Too darn expensive. Gotta pay for absolutly everything no matter if its a tekken original character. Worst part you cant even get the characters using in game currency or in a battle pass. Straight uop greedy developers.
Absolutely not recommended if you are looking for a high skill high reward type of fighting game... full of flowcharts and autopilot players on broken characters...

1. It's fighting game for people who hate fighting games.
2. It's a full priced game with microtransactions.
3. Ruined the good name of TEKKEN.
Don't waste your money.

I am not new but a returning customer to the franchise. I refrained from buying the game for so long because of the reviews and I regret it. I should've got it when it came out. Although it is different, I used to play Tekken 7 on console. I proceeded to take a long break from fighters alike. Despite the 'mixed' reviews I agree that the DLC's are a little bit on the pricey end, but otherwise the game itself is good. 

The people leaving the bad reviews that pertain to gameplay cant swallow the pill that this game rewards players with hyper aggressive styles of gameplay. I personally love it. This game is BY FAR the best fighting game of the new gen competitors. 

TLDR: DO NOT LISTEN TO THE REVIEWS: THE GAME IS GOOD! IF YOU WANT TO PLAY IT, THEN PLAY IT

Currently 15 hours in.

- I can say that i like the game in its current state, i've played about 70+ matches on rank already and noticed that there is no really significant character balance issues on my end ( I currently play Lars, Claudio, Alisa) although that might be because my rank is not high yet.

- There were some connection / stutter issues when playing online, but both players can feel it when it is happening

- Haven't played the main story line yet so I cant say anything about it.

- My gaming laptop is Mid, but I Haven't experience any lag (though my specs are set as Medium and rendering is at 100) 

- Learning combos are satisfying, and skill ceiling is high; you do need to watch youtube videos to really get to know the game mechanics like frames and such, but as a new player of an fighting game genre, i did enjoy the process of trial and error of learning. 

- 8/10 would recommend this game to other people

The game has been improving ever so slowly since the disaster last year and with the Kunimitsu release, I decided to come back. This is still truly the only fighting game I have ever truly enjoyed and nothing else quite hits like it. The game has been on an upwards trajectory for a while now and at this point the "mixed" overall review score is simply not warranted, so I decided to leave a positive review.

Tekken 8 is healing with two consistent great patches. 
Casually this is the best Tekken game and removed some major barriers to entry for anyone who wants to get into the game competitively.
Any flaws within this game, are present in any other major studio fighting game.  
The future is looking bright for this game and there is no better time to give it a shot.
(300+ hours on PS5)

This game makes my blood pressure rise, every waking moment I hop on ranked theres monkeys in my game. Jin, Hwaorang, Eddie, and Steve all monkeys with no thought behind the screen. The only redeeming quality about this game is the fact that Reina and Kunimitsu are both baddies

As a Tekken instalment it's fun enough, the arcade campaign is nice and I liked the visual style. it's great having all the music and checking out old favorites in the story with the nonsensical canon.

The game systems themselves just don't feel good though, and only got worse after the initial season. It just feels like a weak entry when your actually playing matches. Still enjoyable but wait for sales.

I just no longer feel these series is for me. Extreme, non stop aggression, homogenized cast, wonky balance, core dev team straight up leaving. If you're itching for a 3D fighter maybe wait for the new Virtua Fighter or DOA. This is just ain't it anymore.
Year three has started reverting the dumb stuff from year two. its actually worth buying and playing now!

Get ready for the next battle!!!! bro, I bought the game during this era where everyone is complaining about the balance issue but Yujiro Hanma was announced, he's gonna be a DLC,  so yeah, none of it matters as long as I can play as baki's father soon, lol. Good ahh game, good ahh tekken , but indeed some characters are super cheap and needs to be nerfed.
i hate being punished for having a slightly defensive style. this is just a gimmicky juggle fest. barely tekken anymore. time to start remastering the old ones hahaha.
Took namco long enough but the game is getting better. if u want to get this game on deck, it is not good without lossless scaling. otherwise it will run at 40-50fps
It is not perfect, but still the best 3D fighter and it has been improving over the last 2 patches.

If you’re new to fighting games or to Tekken 8, this game is fantastic.

It’s a lot of fun to just hit buttons and see what every character does. You can stumble into some really cool combos even with only a very basic understanding of a character’s moveset. Everything looks cool and feels satisfying to pull off.

The game also has a massive starting roster compared to other fighting games. Street Fighter 6 launched with 18 characters, Guilty Gear -Strive- launched with 15, while Tekken 8 launched with 32. Unlike a lot of other fighting games, DLC doesn’t feel mandatory because 32 characters is already more than enough variety to last hundreds of hours before you even think about spending extra money.

It includes a cinematic story mode and fully animated character arcade endings, both of which are funny and really well done. Tekken has always had some of the best story modes in fighting games because they’re cinematic without taking themselves too seriously, and Tekken 8 continues that perfectly. It also has Arcade Quest, which might honestly be one of the best learning modes ever made for a fighting game. It teaches you the complexities of the system and the characters without feeling like you’re just grinding through tutorials.

On top of that, it has the best character customization in any fighting game right now. It’s built on Unreal Engine 5, so the graphics look amazing, and the netcode is probably best-in-class for a 3D fighting game.

This is objectively a phenomenal fighting game, which is exactly why people are so mad about certain design decisions (and why the reviews are mixed).

People are frustrated with the state of Tekken 8 because it’s such an incredible game — honestly one of the best games of the decade regardless of genre — that gets held back by some really questionable balancing decisions. Tekken 8 is probably the most complete fighting game package ever released, and with just a few relatively minor tweaks, it could easily be the undisputed GOAT.

Instead, every major update feels like the balancing team sat down and mathematically calculated the worst possible design choices they could make before implementing them. It’s like someone making the greatest pizza slice you’ve ever had and then dumping anchovies all over it. Which is why the reaction has been so negative (hence the mixed rating). The emotional response to someone throwing soup on a Picasso is a lot stronger than someone throwing soup on a high school art project.

That said, until you’ve put at least 100 hours into the game, you probably won’t even really notice the impact of these balance changes. For most new players, it’s virtually unnoticeable.

TL;DR: Tekken 8 is a phenomenal game and maybe the best fighting game ever made for the first 100–200 hours. After that, some really questionable and frustrating balancing decisions start becoming noticeable.

always been a tekken fan, but 8 stopped it for me
Worst tekken in the series, bloated systems and ugly aestaetics.
same dlc style as tekken 7

Alright, strap in. This is a long one and you’ll realize why it had to be this long by the end of this review.

For the record, I played Tekken 3 onwards without taking it seriously. Just mashing buttons with friends, having fun, maybe learn a basic combo. Tekken 8 was the first time I actually wanted to learn the game properly. I wanted to learn movement, spacing, punishment, matchup knowledge, all the things longtime players always talk about when they say “real Tekken.”

And after spending hundreds of hours trying to learn it seriously, I came out of it seriously frustrated and depressed.

The ranked climb in this game is one of the most miserable Sisyphean experiences I’ve had in a fighting  game. See you can lose and go “Oh okay I did some mistakes there, I’ll go to the replays and learn”, but most of the characters make you lose for guessing their stance rushdown wrong. Basically, you play the casino and hope you guess them right, which is most definitely what a fighting game is all about. /s

Four points from which the misery comes from:
-	Why bother learning neutral when the game keeps introducing new moves that skip neutral entirely
-	Why bother learning proper punishment and movement when characters are given tools that cover multiple options at once (homing mid strings that is +8 on block and wallsplats and puts character into stance mixup which you have to guess again to not get ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ blown up to pieces)
-	Learning difficult execution only to realize some characters get comparable or better reward from much easier tools (If this is any indication, Devil Jin has been nerfed to graveyard tier in this game. And no Mishima characters are top tier.)
-	Dealing with a system level mechanic that massively rewards momentum and nonstop braindead monkey like offense.

And this is where Heat becomes the main problem. Heat was supposed encourage players to be more aggressive. On paper, it's a good addition. In reality, it's an absolute pain to be on the receiving end of.
Idt aggression is the problem here. Old Tekken games had it's own version of aggression. The problem is that Heat gives too many things at once:
•	free pressure, 
•	chip damage, 
•	forced guesses, 
•	enhanced moves, 
•	heat smashes that does close to combo damage, 
•	combo extensions, 
•	heat dashes, 
•	and ways to stay plus while continuing offense.

Most heat moves have very little counterplay. You just have to eat it and pray to whichever god you worship you survive the ♥♥♥♥ the other player is about to serve you.
Some characters (Steve, Dragunov, King, Lydia, Law) genuinely feel designed around never letting the other player play the game once momentum starts. In fact I think they purposely made EVERYONE stance rushdown character to fit into the Heat meta, which just makes everyone so... similar. There's no character identity anymore. There's no more counter hit focused characters. There's no more spacing and punish focused characters. It's just sad.

You spend time learning movement and spacing just to run into:
•	heat engager into forced stance mixup, 
•	plus frame stance transitions, 
•	tracking mids that catch sidesteps, 
•	armored approaches, 
•	absurd wall carry, 
•	and massive damage off mistakes that don't even feel particularly hard to execute.

And the frustrating part is that this isn't happening occasionally. This happens in every match you play.
People keep saying “just learn the matchup,” but eventually you realize a lot of matchups are less about adaptation and more about memorizing layers upon layers of knowledge checks while dealing with constant forced offense. So if you're a new serious Tekken player, god help you.

A lot of rounds don't feel earned anymore. They feel snowballed.
One mistake becomes launcher into huge wall carry into wall pressure with heat mix. Usually you eat a stupid heat stance mix and eat ♥♥♥♥ at 15 seconds into the round.

And because offense is so overwhelming in this game, sometimes it genuinely feels like you stopped playing 10 seconds ago and you're just waiting to die because the momentum system already decided the round.

The saddest part is that underneath all of this, there actually is a great fighting game here.
There are moments where both players are moving carefully, checking spacing, whiff punishing properly, adapting to habits, conditioning each other. In those moments, the game feels incredible. You finally understand why people fell in love with Tekken in the first place.

But those moments feel interrupted constantly by mechanics that heavily reward forcing offense over carefully building advantages.
And honestly, that's what disappoints me the most.
I wanted to love this game. I spent hundreds of hours trying to love this game. I genuinely tried to improve instead of blaming the game immediately.
But after enough hours, enough ranked sessions, enough labbing, and enough watching entire rounds disappear because someone activated Heat and started running stance pressure, I just don't think the developers actually want newer players to learn Tekken fundamentals properly anymore.
It feels like they want players constantly pressing buttons, constantly engaging, constantly forcing momentum, because that's more exciting to watch. Definitely fun to watch, absolutely hair ripping anger inducing to survive.

Look, maybe that's what you want. Maybe all you want is to win with little to no learning of the game's techniques and letting your character do all the heavy lifting while your brain shuts down looking at all the pretty flashy move effects they put in.

For me, it just signals the death of Tekken as we know it.

I like tekken, this is not authentic tekken. I feel like I'm playing against a disabled rat that is always spamming buttons and the game devs make characters that help with that. Take off the heat system. 

Armor King ARPHFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF

It's a love/hate relationship.
25% love
75% hate
I don't think this game will attract any newcoming players, regarding current season 3. I will say it's visually one the best looking 3D fighting games out there. Current game mechanic system isn't too bad.
The game is pretty expensive if you didn't buy it around launch date. On days where there's no sale deal, you're looking at $120-150. This doesn't factor in the Tekken Fight Pass premium rewards (which are gone forever if you did not unlock it during its brief seasonal period), or the customization DLC and legacy costumes (that can only be unlocked by spending actual money on "Tekken Coins").
This game clears Tekken 7 in every way idk why people are mad, its got better stages, great character models and better music
the balancing in actual gameplay is ♥♥♥♥♥♥. the available basegame character skins are mid and the customization options are barely decent.

After 200 solid hours of playing this game I can confidently say I 100% recommend it. For the longest time there has been so much negativity around this game and it was the main component in causing me and many others to afford it out of fear of wasting money and time but after many patches the game and community are in a much better place now and slowly but surely Tekken 8 is becoming a game new and old players love. Of course you will always outspoken people who spew venom but as long as you dont doom Q and dont take it TOO seriously(like youre a pro or something lol) you too can have a lot of fun

In general every character controls as smoothly as they look and those that represent a martial art do it in the best  way possible with real techniques + even some personal moves that fit that character in particular, you see this a lot in Hwoarang, Steve and both Kings. 

Runs extremely smooth, crisp graphics and it looks beautiful. A minor but much welcome addition is the ability to customize the music of the game so you can make it sound like any TEKKEN EVER MADE. Maybe my one complaint is there I wish there were more classic(legacy) free customes for more characters as those past games had a very distinct fashion that unfortunately doesnt return. Regardless though there plenty unique player made customs so plenty of room for creativity

This is probably the best Tekken I've played so far in terms of online connectivity, however there's way too much bs and fluff added in compared to the previous games, even moreso than Tekken 7 where most of the heat animations or whatever the kids are calling it these days are long enough for you to get up, take a piss, and get back into the match no time flat. It's difficult for me to recommend this for anyone wanting to get into/back into Tekken but it's worth picking up when it's on special, much like what I did :P

fighting is just as good as all the other tekkens, definitely has a ton of ways of countering in the game plus the music gets you motivated while fighting. The story mode is perfect and glad they had a clip per each tekken so your not like lost in the game at all.

Only bought the game for Yujiro and because it was on sale. Charging $35 for 3 characters in a video game that itself costs $40 is absolutely ludicrous and you all have done that 3 times. Insanity. 1 character available to unlock upon game completion. 1. Bandai Namco, blow me.

I've never thought I'd get into a fighting game and actually liking it and at the same time hating it. Players here are very very competitive! And I don't like getting beat up by some dudes so my competitive sprit spiked up and got me consistently playing it. Also the combo's are freaking hard but once you learn it, it's like busting a nut ngl. Great game!
Easier combos and anime like graphics. This game is awesome.

Started Playing Tekken at Tekken 2 every game has felt different from the last. That being said I have really enjoyed T8 and love the style and just wish they stopped with recent characters and brought back older chars like Roger and Alex, Wang (I know he is dead so was Heihachi like 3 times...), Bruce, Beak, and Jinpachi. 

And for those complaining about the difference I legit feel like the jump from Tekken 3 to 4 was WAY more different.
Either way the game is fun and better than most fighting games out right now.
You have to keep buying all the content over and over for each season. Instead of buying one bundle for anything and future releases. Pretty damn greedy for a game that is fun.

its truly a miracle how you can make the game feel completely different in vs AI, private match and ranked. I can be doing combos easily vs AI and private and somehow the second ranked is touched the game feels completely different. where is the consistency?
Recent update keeps overclocking gpu and shutting down display requiring a full reset. This is the only game causing this issue.

if you are here to have fun with friends, 100% 

if you are here for a competitively balanced and engaging game, probably pass for now. Lotta roulette, plus frames into stance into guess for game in many situations.

love this game. super fun. needs fixing, feel bad for ppl who make money off this game rn. But as somebody who is just getting into fighting games. This is my fav series 4 sure, not my fav tekken i played but up there 4 sure. Over hated imo.
love this game effects are hella cool, Better than ur average pvp game but a lil bit pricey

its an amazing game i do really enjoy i hope you find this review helpful PS: if you rage be prepared for that as this game is rage inducing a little to some so just be prepared for that other than that this game is fun and really cool
Game has its issues, but I feel most of peoples complaints are exaggerated. Fun fighting game

While I still have certain griped with the game, admittedly I'm still having a lot of fun with my friends when it comes to Tekken 8. Rollback is nice, the way it teaches you basics feels nice, and the Arcade Quest, despite feeling at first like it wasn't good to play at first, it does try to teach core mechanics based on who your playing with.

Personally, I still wish the story mode would allow you to play with the character you want while still getting to see cutscenes of the Mishima and the Kazama storytelling, But I get it, they want us to experience what they character must have gone through as well. And I suppose they succeeded, just took me a bit to learn the character (But to be fair, I did play it on hard instead of easy, so it's a bit on me). Meanwhile, everyone else doesn't really get much story other than like, minor parts of the story or its own Character Episode. Meaning we have to continue paying attention to interviews and shadow dropped lore on wikis for anything else.

The gameplay in and of itself is fun. And while it was rough from the get go, the controls has been simplified a lot since back then. Maybe a bit too simplified, but it's definitely very beginner friendly. From a casual perspective, it's nice if your aim is only to play for fun and not competitive. But there will be people who abuse this in competitive play surely. Other than that, all in all it can be pretty fun if you know when to take a break, desalt, and other stuff.

I do wish there was much more for customization on base game and not rely so much on battle pass. Or at the very least, if you will make battle pass exclusive items, please make sure they come back or something. As someone who joined late because I was unavailable, really wanted to get the "I hate Rich People" shirt, but it doesn't look like I can get it anymore. Not to mention, base game customization just looks so... empty compared to Tekken 5 through 7. Tekken 6 and 7 (Don't you dare make the joke) had so much customization. So much, that the optimal way to get more item was via Treasure Battle in Tekken 7, and even then, half the time, it might not be for the character you play. In here? You get the bare minimum. Want more? Invest some money in battle pass to get unlocked customization. Missed it? Sucks to be you. Battle pass just in general sucks.

Finally, the Tekken Longue and Ghost Battle. Honestly? These modes are pretty useful. Back then we only had lobby rooms back in the days. And if you weren't someone with Keyboard adapters back in console, there was little reason to use the player lobby chat box. But in Fighter Lounge where multiple people are around? It feels so natural to chat and learn with other players when attempting to play for fun or even just get to socialize with other Tekken fans. I don't think I ever socialize with other Tekken fans online. Mainly because I was worried everyone was competitive hungry but, I guess some of them are relatable to the characters in Arcade Quest. Which is a nice breath of fresh air. Some people focus more on communicating by customization, how others play, and sometimes, expressing with favorite moves of their characters. And I appreciate that. All while the Ghost mode also gives you a good idea of how those players play and potentially practice with data similar to said players. Of course,I wouldn't say Ghost Data will replace players. In my opinion, Ghost Data will play a little worse than what the actual player can truly do. But it is nice to have something to practice with.

All in all, despite I have a preference for other Tekken games, Tekken 8 is fun to play and communicate with still. Despite the things that does disappoint me.

This game provides a thoroughly engaging experience. This installation in the Tekken series provides a well rounded cast of fighters, featuring iconic characters such as Jin, Kazuya and Nina. I loved the addition of Jun Kazama after missing out on previous games. The new characters are refreshing and feel unique from the rest of the cast. Tekken 8 is packed full of entertaining offline game modes. My personal favourite being Arcade Quest. I derive satisfaction from gaining new titles for my character as I persevere through tough fights. Tekken Ball also provides mild amusement. I also adore the arcade battle mode in the game. It feels worth overcoming the final boss fight to see the end of each characters story in amusing cutscenes that are indicative of Tekken's distinctive quirky humour. The combat system  accommodates inexperienced players for who this game is their first interaction with Tekken. The rage meter adds exciting tension to each battle and forces you to strategize each move carefully. I overall recommend this game to anyone new to the fighting game genre and to seasoned professionals of Tekken.
I liked tekken 7 but this game just feels like constant attack spam and seems to punish you for trying to counter strike in between combos

Absolutely amazing entry into tekken.

Now I do wanna preface everything with this is a wildly different Tekken, it plays more like smash brothers in where everyone has the same basic directional attacks, as well as heats and rage. That makes the game more welcoming to new plays. The good news though is for the players who have been playing tekken since the 90's, all the combos we know are still in this tekken but the inputs and entries may be a little different. So old heads arent completely left in the dust, needing to learn a new game. The game was had some lows though, season 2 truly did blow, season 3 isn't perfect but its a good change but the devs are still gonna have to level up to making 8 as good as 7.

PC Optimization is also really amazing, you just need to use the right upscaling protocol for your system or just not use scaling, but game is beautiful, and runs at any monitor refresh rate, and has native ultrawide support. On a technical level its amazing. Meta, just know what you are getting into, its never been totally broken like DOA or other fighters so that is good.
By no means am i any good in the game ,but it is fun ,as long as you have some friends, that can give you pointers on what not to do.
Played this on ps5, Runs even better on pc, No issues, legit just Tekken, Nothing more to it. Great game! :D

Mixed leaning on positive for me. It's a toss up whether matches feel like good ol' tekken or like fighting a slot machine with stance slop. I still have fun with the game, but when you see people that still use the assist menu all the way up in GoD ranks....you know something is wonky. You can master your character, punishes etc. and still get beat by someone unga bungaing their forehead on their controller their 2nd week of playing. It definitely takes some adapting to new mechanics, but there's still a lot of fun to be had with this game.
as someone who has a lot of memories with tekken 3, this is absolutely amazing. i have played almost all tekken games, but i haven't enjoyed one this much since tekken 3. so fu*king epic...

The online play, even on quick play, is infested with script/macro cheaters even at beginner rank. You'll essentially never see anyone drop a single combo because their software is doing it for them. They'll magically block every high/low mix up and duck every grab. There are entire youtube channels dedicated to pointing out Tekken 8 cheaters. I bet most of the Tekken 8 youtubers are also cheating just to keep up. I've never seen anything so toxic in my entire 30+ years of gaming and there's literally no point in playing if you just want to have fun playing against rando's online.

The possibility of being asexual is something I've struggled with for years.  I've spent several sleepless nights staring at the ceiling and wondering if I'll leave this world having never experienced an entire dimension of life because of how the neurons happened to connect themselves in my brain.  I've questioned my identity for a long time now, flip-flopping between calling myself asexual and gay and loathing myself for not being able to pick a lane and make things simple again.

These feelings are more pleasant than most matches of TEKKEN 8.  Do NOT spend forty dollars on a game that will make you question your sense of self like I did.

I only have around 10 hours at the time of writing this, so take this as an early impression, not a 500-hour ranked veteran essay.

Tekken 8 looks great. The presentation is easily one of its strongest points: the characters look good, the hits feel heavy, the stages are pretty, and as a single-player/casual offline game it can genuinely feel awesome.

But the more I touch online, the more the game starts to feel wrong to me.

The problem is not that Tekken 8 lacks effort. The problem is the direction. Online often feels like a mashy 50/50 blender where every character has stance pressure, Heat pressure, forced mixups, plus frames, or some “guess now” situation. Instead of feeling like I’m slowly learning a deep fighting game, it often feels like I’m being shoved into casino situations until I guess wrong and explode.

Ranked/casual online can feel miserable because you never really know what you’re running into: someone smurfing, someone cheating, someone mashing, someone using Special Style, or someone who already knows every obscure interaction while you’re still trying to breathe.

I understand Special Style exists for accessibility, and I’m not saying using it is cheating. But in a game that already feels very aggressive and simplified around offense, it adds to the feeling that Tekken 8 is moving away from the fundamentals I respected: movement, spacing, punishment, defense, patience, and learning why you lost.

The DLC/business direction does not help either. This is a full-price game that still sells characters and cosmetics on top. Some returning legacy characters are being packaged as paid DLC, which feels bad when these are characters people already associate with Tekken’s history. Add the shop/pass structure and it starts feeling less like a premium fighting game and more like another live-service product wearing a Tekken mask.

The Season 2 backlash also makes the whole thing feel worse, because many players were already asking for better defense and less forced offense, and the criticism was that the game doubled down on the exact things people disliked.

And with Harada leaving Bandai Namco after so many years, the future of the series feels uncertain. Maybe that brings needed change. Maybe not. But right now, Tekken 8 feels like a beautiful game with a confused soul.

I do not hate it. I can see the talent in it. Offline, it can be fun.

But online, it too often feels like a polished, expensive, stance-heavy 50/50 pressure machine.

Final verdict: Beautiful visuals, fun offline, frustrating online, too much forced offense, too much monetization, and not enough of the restraint and fundamentals that made Tekken feel like Tekken.

EDIT: Also, i relate with the person who wrote a review saying "the feeling of freeing up disc space when you uninstall it is more enjoyable than the gameplay"

I've sunk a lot of time into this game on playstation, but am updating my rig so I thought I'd get it here and progress through the ranks again since I'm abandoning my ps. 

Remove rage art and make the neutral game matter and this would honestly be the best fighter on the market. 400+ combos per character, yet we are still favouring button mashing one based combo aggression. It was beautiful when defence meant something in the older tekken titles. The aggression based meta is becoming extremely boring and is removing all of the skill expression from the game. Bring back old footsies gameplay and it'll be at its peak again.

The new update with Kunimitsu is incredibly good and very fun. I just hope next season since mid year couldn't even fix it the competitive direction of the game will head into the right direction. Ranked doesn't matter anymore and we are solely playing this because mk1 is dead and heat in sf6 is terrible.

As much as I genuinely love the gameplay and base game, I can not recommend this game until their microtransactions are toned down. They charge way too much, and you can't even learn DLC characters movesets in practice vs AI without owning them. If the game is on sale with a full/ultimate package where you get all the DLC with the game, get it. Otherwise I'd avoid it.

I only discovered fighting games in now that I'm in my 30s, and have fallen in love with all sorts of 3D fighters, watching FGC tournaments, the works. Tekken 8 has been a great fighter to choose as my main. It's Hype AF and I've had a blast labbing all the characters, and getting local friends into it with me.

It's not perfect: the combos can last upwards of 10 seconds, and the heat system and rage arts can extend that feeling of having your turn denied. But no other fighter rewards you for honing it like Tekken does, and playing Tekken 8 is like jumping into a wrestling promo that gives you a prime time show every single night.
